title: SIP, lumpsum & averaging — what actually helps
intro: Untangle the myth that a SIP magically beats a lumpsum.

steps.title: What rupee-cost averaging really does
steps.body:
A fixed monthly amount buys **more units when prices are low** and fewer when high, smoothing your average cost. That genuinely reduces the risk of dumping everything at a peak.

But markets rise more often than they fall — so money invested *earlier* is exposed to more growth time. Averaging manages **regret and timing risk**, not a guaranteed return premium.

steps.title: Watch the compounding
steps.body: The power of a SIP is consistency over time, not a trick. Drag the sliders — most of the final corpus is compounding, not your contributions.

viz.title: Future value of a monthly SIP

steps.title: So which should you use?
steps.body:
> [!warn] "SIP always beats lumpsum" is a myth. If you already hold the cash, delaying it via a SIP usually *lowers* the outcome in a rising market.

- **Monthly salary** → SIP (matches cash flow, builds the habit).
- **A windfall** → invest sooner, or phase it in with an **STP** (Systematic Transfer Plan) if a peak worries you.

The SIP's real edge is behavioural: you'll actually stick to it.

recap:
**SIP vs lumpsum answer different questions.**

- Averaging smooths cost and reduces timing regret — it is not a return premium.
- Have cash now? Investing sooner usually wins; an STP phases it in gently.
- Pick the plan you will *stick to*, not the back-tested ideal.
